html:
[807]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 王母第二十三女 - Literal translation: 'the 23rd daughter of the Queen Mother'; refers to a mythological figure in Chinese folklore.
  • 西华少阴之气 - Refers to a specific type of cosmic energy in Chinese cosmology.
  • 尝东海游 - Means 'once traveled to the East Sea'; indicates a journey.
  • 还过江上 - Means 'returned and passed by the river'; describes a return journey.
  • 峰岩挺拔 - Describes the landscape as 'peak and rock towering'.
  • 林壑幽丽 - Describes the forest and valleys as 'secluded and beautiful'.
  • 巨石如坛 - Means 'large stones like altars'; describes the landscape.
  • 留连久之 - Means 'lingered for a long time'; indicates a prolonged stay.
  • 时大禹理水 - Means 'at the time when the Great Yu was managing the waters'; refers to a historical event.
  • 驻山下 - Means 'stayed at the foot of the mountain'; describes the location.
  • 大风卒至 - Means 'a great wind suddenly arrived'; describes a sudden event.
  • 崖振谷陨 - Means 'the cliffs shook and the valleys collapsed'; describes the aftermath of the wind.
  • 不可制 - Means 'could not be controlled'; indicates the severity of the event.
  • 因与夫人相值 - Means 'thus met with the lady'; describes an encounter.
  • 拜而求助 - Means 'bowed and sought help'; describes the action taken.
  • 即敕侍女授禹 - Means 'immediately ordered the maidservant to give Yu'; describes the action taken.
  • 策召鬼神之书 - Means 'the book that summons ghosts and spirits'; refers to a magical text.
  • 因命其神狂章，虞余，黄魔，大翳，庚辰，童律等 - Means 'thus commanded the spirits named Kuang Zhang, Yu Yu, Huang Mo, Da Yi, Geng Chen, and Tong Lu'; lists the spirits.
  • 助禹斫石疏波，决塞道厄，以循其流 - Means 'to assist Yu in cutting stones, clearing the waves, breaking through obstacles, and following the flow'; describes the actions taken.
  • 禹拜而谢焉 - Means 'Yu bowed and thanked'; describes the response.
  • 禹尝诣之崇巘之巅 - Means 'Yu once went to the top of the high peak'; describes the location.
  • 顾盼之际，化而为石 - Means 'in the blink of an eye, transformed into stone'; describes a transformation.
  • 其后，楚大夫宋玉，以其事言于襄王 - Means 'later, the Chu official Song Yu told this story to King Xiang'; describes a historical account.
  • 王作阳台之宫以祀之 - Means 'the king built the Yangtai Palace to worship her'; describes a historical action.
  • 隔岸有神女之石，即所化也 - Means 'on the opposite bank, there is a stone of the goddess, which is the transformed form'; describes a geographical feature.
  • 神女坛侧，有竹垂垂若篲 - Means 'near the goddess's altar, there are bamboo stalks hanging like brooms'; describes a natural feature.
  • 有槁叶飞物着坛上者，竹则因风扫之，终莹洁不为所污 - Means 'if dead leaves or flying objects touch the altar, the bamboo sweeps them away with the wind, always keeping it clean and unstained'; describes a natural phenomenon.
  • 李白感兴诗云 - Means 'Li Bai's poem inspired by this event says'; introduces a poetic reference.
  • 瑶姬天帝女，精彩化朝云 - Means 'Yaoji, the daughter of the Heavenly Emperor, transformed into morning clouds'; describes a mythological transformation.
  • 宛转入宵梦，无心向楚君 - Means 'gracefully entering the night dream, without any intention towards the king of Chu'; describes a mythological action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a prominent figure in Chinese mythology.
  • 大禹 - Refers to the legendary Chinese leader who managed the great flood.
  • 楚大夫宋玉 - Refers to Song Yu, a famous poet and statesman of the State of Chu during the Warring States period.
  • 襄王 - Refers to King Xiang of Chu, a historical figure.
  • 李白 - Refers to Li Bai, a renowned Tang dynasty poet.
  • 瑶姬 - Refers to a mythological figure, often associated with the Queen Mother of the West.
  • 天帝女 - Refers to the daughter of the Heavenly Emperor, a common motif in Chinese mythology.
  • 精彩化朝云 - Refers to the transformation of a mythological figure into morning clouds, a common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 宛转入宵梦，无心向楚君 - Refers to the mythological figure's actions in a dream, without any intention towards the king of Chu.

Proper Nouns

  • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
  • 大禹 - The legendary Chinese leader who managed the great flood.
  • 楚大夫宋玉 - Song Yu, a famous poet and statesman of the State of Chu.
  • 襄王 - King Xiang of Chu.
  • 李白 - Li Bai, a renowned Tang dynasty poet.
  • 瑶姬 - A mythological figure.
  • 天帝女 - The daughter of the Heavenly Emperor.
  • 西华少阴之气 - A specific type of cosmic energy.
  • 巫山 - A mountain in China, known for its scenic beauty.
  • 崇巘 - A high peak.
  • 阳台之宫 - The Yangtai Palace.
  • 神女之石 - The stone of the goddess.
  • 神女坛 - The goddess's altar.
  • 篲 - A broom.
  • 朝云 - Morning clouds.
  • 楚君 - The king of Chu.

[808]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 《襄阳耆旧传》云 - Indicates a quotation from the *Xiangyang Qi Jiu Zhuan* (Records of the Ancient and Virtuous of Xiangyang).
  • 《云溪友议》云 - Indicates a quotation from *Yunxi Youyi* (Discussions of the Cloud Stream).
  • 昼梦宵征 - Describes dreaming during the day and night, suggesting a continuous state of dreaming.
  • 非真梦也 - Directly states that the dream was not real.
  • 李公大惭 - Describes Li Deyu feeling ashamed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 楚襄王 - King Xiang of Chu, a ruler of the Chu state during the Warring States period.
  • 云梦 - The Yunmeng Marsh, a famous wetland in ancient China.
  • 瑶姬 - A mythical figure often associated with the Lady of Wushan.
  • 夏帝 - The Xia Emperor, a legendary ruler of the Xia dynasty.
  • 巫山 - A mountain range in Hubei Province, known for its association with the Lady of Wushan.
  • 阳台 - Yuhang Terrace, a location on Wushan.
  • 赤帝 - The Red Emperor, a mythical ruler associated with fire and the south.
  • 太尉李德裕 - Li Deyu, a prominent statesman and poet during the Tang dynasty.
  • 渚宫 - Zhubao Palace, a palace in the Tang dynasty.
  • 宋玉 - A famous poet and statesman during the Warring States period.
  • 屈平 - Qu Yuan, a renowned poet and statesman during the Warring States period.

Proper Nouns

  • 《襄阳耆旧传》 - Title of the historical record *Xiangyang Qi Jiu Zhuan* (Records of the Ancient and Virtuous of Xiangyang).
  • 《云溪友议》 - Title of the collection of anecdotes and discussions *Yunxi Youyi* (Discussions of the Cloud Stream).
  • 太尉李德裕 - Full name of Li Deyu, a prominent statesman and poet during the Tang dynasty.
  • 渚宫 - Full name of Zhubao Palace, a palace in the Tang dynasty.
  • 宋玉 - Full name of Song Yu, a famous poet and statesman during the Warring States period.
  • 屈平 - Full name of Qu Yuan, a renowned poet and statesman during the Warring States period.

[809]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 忽聞 - Suddenly heard, introduces a supernatural element.
  • 其寢臥服玩之物，俱非世有 - The objects for sleeping and playing, all were not of this world, emphasizes the otherworldly nature of the items.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 建業 - Modern-day Nanjing, a major city during the Southern Dynasties.
  • 江陵 - Modern-day Jingzhou, another significant city during the Southern Dynasties.
  • 宋廢帝元徽中 - The Yuanhuai era (473–477) of Emperor Wu of Song, a period marked by political turmoil.
  • 明月峡 - A specific location in the story, likely a scenic gorge.
  • 神女 - A divine or supernatural female figure, often associated with mountains or natural features.
  • 上帝 - The Heavenly Emperor, the supreme deity in Chinese mythology.
  • 三百年一易 - A reference to the cyclical nature of divine existence, with a period of 300 years.

Proper Nouns

  • 《八朝窮怪錄》 - A title of a classical text, likely a collection of strange or unusual stories from the Eight Dynasties.
  • 蕭總 - The protagonist's name, Xiao Zong.
  • 宋廢帝 - Emperor Wu of Song.
  • 元徽 - The era name of Emperor Wu of Song (473–477).
  • 明月峡 - A specific location in the story.
  • 神女 - A divine or supernatural female figure.
  • 上帝 - The Heavenly Emperor.

[810]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 謂曰 - A formal way of introducing direct speech, meaning 'said to.'
  • 此妾尝服玩，未曾离手 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'this is something I have worn and cherished, never letting it out of my hands.'
  • 愿郎穿指，慎勿忘心 - Request meaning 'I wish for you to pierce my finger, but please do not forget my heart.'
  • 幸见顾录，感恨徒深 - Expression of gratitude and sorrow, meaning 'I am fortunate to be noticed and recorded, my feelings of gratitude and sorrow are profound.'
  • 执此怀中，终身是宝 - Statement of affection and value, meaning 'holding this in my heart, it will be my treasure for life.'
  • 天渐明 - Phrase indicating the passage of time, meaning 'the sky gradually brightened.'
  • 总乃拜辞，掩涕而别 - Formal farewell with tears, meaning 'Zong then bowed and bid farewell, wiping away tears.'
  • 携手出户，已见路径分明 - Description of leaving, meaning 'holding hands and stepping out the door, the path was already clear.'
  • 总下数步，回顾宿处 - Description of looking back, meaning 'Zong took a few steps down, looking back at the place where he stayed.'
  • 宛见巫山神女之祠也 - Description of a place, meaning 'he saw the shrine of the Goddess of Mount Wuqiu.'
  • 他日持玉环至建业，因话于张景山 - Recounting of a future event, meaning 'one day, holding the jade ring, he went to Jianye and recounted the story to Zhang Jingshan.'
  • 吾尝游巫峡，见神女乞后玉环 - Recounting of a past experience, meaning 'I once traveled through the Wu Gorge and saw the Goddess of the Gorge asking for a jade ring after her death.'
  • 觉后乃告帝，帝遣使赐神女 - Recounting of a divine intervention, meaning 'after her death, she reported to the Emperor, who sent an envoy to grant the Goddess a jade ring.'
  • 吾亲见在神女指上，今卿得之是矣 - Confirmation of a divine object, meaning 'I personally saw it on the Goddess's finger, so now you have it.'
  • 齐太祖建元末方征召，未行 - Historical reference, meaning 'at the end of the Jianyuan period of the Northern Qi dynasty, he was summoned but did not go.'
  • 帝崩，世祖即位，累为中书舍人 - Historical reference, meaning 'the emperor died, and the successor ascended the throne, repeatedly appointing him as a secretary of the central government.'
  • 初，总为制书御史，江陵舟中，偶思神女事，悄然不乐 - Personal reflection, meaning 'initially, when Zong was a censor in charge of imperial edicts, on a boat in Jingling, he suddenly thought of the Goddess and felt melancholic.'
  • 乃赋诗曰 - Introduction to a poem, meaning 'he then composed a poem saying.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 巫山神女 - The Goddess of Mount Wuqiu, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology.
  • 建业 - The ancient name for Nanjing, a major city in southern China.
  • 张景山 - Zhang Jingshan, a historical figure mentioned in the text.
  • 齐太祖建元 - The Jianyuan period of the Northern Qi dynasty.
  • 江陵 - The ancient name for Jingzhou, a city in central China.
  • 制书御史 - A censor in charge of imperial edicts, a high-ranking official in the imperial government.
  • 中书舍人 - A secretary of the central government, a high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy.

Proper Nouns

  • 巫山神女 - The Goddess of Mount Wuqiu.
  • 建业 - Nanjing.
  • 张景山 - Zhang Jingshan.
  • 齐太祖建元 - The Jianyuan period of the Northern Qi dynasty.
  • 江陵 - Jingzhou.
  • 制书御史 - A censor in charge of imperial edicts.
  • 中书舍人 - A secretary of the central government.

[811]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 又《三峡记》云 - Introduces a secondary source, the *Three Gorges Record*.
  • 投置船中 - Literal description of placing the fish in the boat.
  • 以草覆之 - Simple action of covering the fish.
  • 见一美女在草下 - Literal description of the transformation.
  • 偶化鱼游 - Poetic way of describing the transformation.
  • 冥契使然 - Indicates predestined fate.
  • 遂为亮妻 - Straightforward description of the outcome.
  • 数已足矣 - Indicates the end of a period.
  • 请归高唐 - Request to return to a place.
  • 情不可忘者 - Indicates deep emotional connection.
  • 有思后至 - Indicates future visits.
  • 忽曰 - Indicates a sudden statement.
  • 不知所终 - Indicates an unknown fate.
  • 不知高唐之女，又是何人也 - Concluding question about the identity of the woman.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三峡 - Refers to the Three Gorges, a famous natural wonder in China.
  • 宋顺帝升平二年 - Refers to the second year of the Shengping era during the reign of Emperor Shun of the Song Dynasty.
  • 微生亮 - Proper name of a person from the story.
  • 高唐 - Place name, possibly referring to a region or a mythological location.
  • 高唐之女 - Refers to a woman from Gao Tang, likely a mythical or legendary figure.
  • 冥契 - Refers to a predestined fate or destiny.
  • 情不可忘者 - Refers to those who cannot forget their feelings, indicating a deep emotional bond.
  • 有思后至 - Refers to those who will come later, indicating future visits.
  • 忽曰 - A phrase indicating a sudden statement, often used in classical texts to describe unexpected actions or words.
  • 不知所终 - A phrase indicating an unknown fate, often used in classical texts to describe the disappearance or unknown outcome of a character.
  • 不知高唐之女，又是何人也 - A concluding question about the identity of the woman from Gao Tang, indicating the mystery surrounding her.

Proper Nouns

  • 三峡 - The Three Gorges.
  • 宋顺帝 - Emperor Shun of the Song Dynasty.
  • 升平二年 - The second year of the Shengping era.
  • 微生亮 - A person from the story.
  • 高唐 - A place name.
  • 高唐之女 - A woman from Gao Tang.
  • 冥契 - A predestined fate or destiny.
  • 情不可忘者 - Those who cannot forget their feelings.
  • 有思后至 - Those who will come later.
  • 忽曰 - A phrase indicating a sudden statement.
  • 不知所终 - An unknown fate.
  • 不知高唐之女，又是何人也 - A concluding question about the identity of the woman.

[812]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不第 - Failed the imperial examinations, indicating the protagonist did not pass the civil service exams.
  • 言词问接 - Communicating indirectly, suggesting a level of formality or distance in their interactions.
  • 同为胡越犹怀想 - Even if we were as far apart as Hu and Yue, I would still think of you, using a historical reference to distant lands to express longing.
  • 愿随鸾鹤入青云 - I wish to follow the phoenix and crane into the blue clouds, expressing a desire to ascend to heaven.
  • 幽栖岩谷 - Retiring to the mountains, a common theme in Chinese literature and philosophy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐长庆中 - Refers to the Longqing era of the Tang Dynasty (821-824 AD).
  • 鄂渚 - An ancient name for the area around modern-day Wuhan.
  • 崔相国 - Refers to Cui, a high-ranking official, likely a chancellor.
  • 胡越 - Refers to distant lands, often used to describe separation or distance.
  • 天仙 - Refers to a celestial beauty, often used to describe an exceptionally beautiful woman.
  • 玉京 - Refers to the Heavenly Capital, a mythical place in Chinese mythology.
  • 鸾鹤 - Refers to the phoenix and crane, symbols of immortality and purity in Chinese culture.
  • 汉南 - Refers to the southern part of the Han River region, likely a reference to a specific location or region.

Proper Nouns

  • 裴航 - The name of the protagonist.
  • 崔相国 - The name of the high-ranking official.
  • 樊夫人 - The name of the lady.
  • 袅烟 - The name of the maid.

[813]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一饮琼浆百感生 - Poetic expression meaning 'One drink of nectar evokes a hundred feelings,' suggesting a profound emotional response to something extraordinary.
  • 玄霜捣尽见云英 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'After pounding the black frost, the cloud maiden appears,' symbolizing the revelation of something pure or divine after effort.
  • 蓝桥便是神仙窟 - Poetic phrase meaning 'The Blue Bridge is the abode of immortals,' referring to a mythical or idyllic place.
  • 何必崎岖上玉清 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why go through hardship to ascend to the Jade Clarity (a high place in Daoist cosmology)?,' suggesting that something desirable is already within reach.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 琼浆 - Refers to nectar, a mythical drink associated with immortality and divine pleasure.
  • 玄霜 - A metaphor for something pure or divine, often associated with Daoist alchemy and the pursuit of immortality.
  • 云英 - A term for a cloud maiden or a pure, ethereal being, often used in Daoist or mythological contexts.
  • 蓝桥 - A reference to the Blue Bridge, a legendary place in Chinese folklore associated with immortals and romantic encounters.
  • 玉清 - A high place in Daoist cosmology, representing a pure and elevated realm.

Proper Nouns

  • 蓝桥 - The Blue Bridge, a legendary place in Chinese folklore.
  • 玉清 - A high place in Daoist cosmology, representing a pure and elevated realm.

[814]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 良久 - Indicates a significant delay, often used in classical literature to build tension or emphasize a moment.
  • 向睹 - Refers to a recent observation, often used to set the scene or introduce a key event.
  • 艷麗惊人 - Describes extreme beauty, often used to highlight the attractiveness of a character.
  • 踌蹰不舍 - Indicates a strong desire to stay, often used to show a character's attachment or reluctance.
  • 纳厚礼娶之 - Refers to a traditional practice of offering a substantial gift to marry someone, often used to show the value placed on a person.
  • 老病 - Describes someone who is elderly and frail, often used to show the character's age and condition.
  • 女孙 - Refers to a granddaughter, often used to show family relationships.
  • 昨有神仙 - Refers to a recent supernatural encounter, often used to introduce magical elements.
  • 遗灵丹一刀圭 - Refers to a small but potent magical medicine, often used to show the power of magic.
  • 玉杵臼 - Refers to a jade mortar and pestle, often used to show the importance of a tool in a magical context.
  • 百日方可就吞 - Indicates a lengthy process, often used to show the complexity of a task.
  • 后天而老 - Refers to living long and remaining young, often used to show the goal of immortality or longevity.
  • 君约取此女者 - Refers to a conditional agreement, often used to show a commitment.
  • 得玉杵臼 - Refers to obtaining a required item, often used to show a prerequisite.
  • 吾当与之 - Refers to a promise, often used to show a commitment.
  • 无用处 - Refers to items that are not needed, often used to show a lack of value.
  • 航拜谢曰 - Refers to a formal expression of gratitude, often used to show respect.
  • 愿以百日为期 - Refers to setting a deadline, often used to show a commitment to a timeline.
  • 必携杵臼至 - Refers to a promise to fulfill a requirement, often used to show a commitment.
  • 更无他许人 - Refers to exclusivity, often used to show a unique opportunity.
  • 恨恨而去 - Refers to leaving angrily, often used to show a strong negative emotion.
  • 及至京国 - Refers to arriving in the capital, often used to show a change of location.
  • 殊不以举事为意 - Refers to not taking a matter seriously, often used to show a lack of urgency.
  • 但于坊曲喧衢 - Refers to a noisy public place, often used to show a bustling environment.
  • 高声访玉杵臼 - Refers to loudly searching for an item, often used to show a public announcement.
  • 曾无影响 - Refers to a lack of response, often used to show a lack of success.
  • 或遇朋友 - Refers to a chance encounter, often used to show a random meeting.
  • 若不相识 - Refers to a lack of recognition, often used to show a misunderstanding.
  • 众言为狂人 - Refers to being called crazy, often used to show a negative perception.
  • 忽遇一货玉老翁 - Refers to a fortuitous encounter, often used to show a lucky break.
  • 近得虢州药铺卞老书云 - Refers to a recent communication, often used to show a new piece of information.
  • 郎君恳求如此 - Refers to a strong desire, often used to show enthusiasm.
  • 吾当为书导达 - Refers to a promise to facilitate a request, often used to show a commitment.
  • 愧荷珍重 - Refers to a formal expression of thanks, often used to show respect.
  • 果获杵臼 - Refers to obtaining an item, often used to show success.
  • 非二百缗不可得 - Refers to a high cost, often used to show the value of an item.
  • 泻囊 - Refers to emptying a purse, often used to show a significant expenditure.
  • 兼货仆马 - Refers to selling additional resources, often used to show a desperate measure.
  • 方及其值 - Refers to covering the cost, often used to show a precise calculation.
  • 遂步骤独挈 - Refers to walking alone, carrying something, often used to show a personal journey.
  • 而抵蓝桥 - Refers to arriving at a destination, often used to show a final location.

[815]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 妪大笑 - Describes an old woman laughing.
  • 有如是信士乎 - Translates to 'Is there such a trustworthy man?'
  • 吾岂爱惜女子，而不酬其劳哉 - Means 'How could I be stingy with a woman and not reward her labor?'
  • 女亦微笑 - Describes a woman smiling.
  • 虽然 - A transitional phrase meaning 'even so' or 'however.'
  • 更为吾捣药百日 - Means 'to grind medicine for me for another hundred days.'
  • 方议姻好 - Translates to 'only then will we discuss marriage.'
  • 航即捣之 - Means 'the man immediately began grinding.'
  • 昼为夜息 - Describes the man working during the day and resting at night.
  • 夜则媪收药臼于内室 - Means 'at night the old woman would take the mortar and pestle into the inner chamber.'
  • 航又闻捣药声 - Describes the man hearing the sound of grinding medicine again.
  • 引航入帐就礼讫 - Means 'the woman led the man into the tent and they completed the ceremony.'
  • 悲泣感荷 - Translates to 'shed tears of gratitude.'
  • 裴郎自是清冷裴真人子孙 - Means 'Pai Lang is a descendant of the pure and cold True Person Pai.'
  • 业当出世 - Translates to 'destined to transcend the world.'
  • 不足深愧老妪也 - Means 'there is no need for me to feel deeply ashamed before this old woman.'
  • 引见诸宾 - Means 'introduced him to all the guests.'
  • 多神仙中人 - Describes many as beings from the realm of immortals.
  • 后有仙女 - Means 'later there was a fairy.'
  • 鬟髻霓衣 - Describes the fairy with hairpins and a rainbow-colored dress.
  • 云是妻姊 - Translates to 'she said she was the woman's sister.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 妪 - Refers to an elderly woman, often used in stories to represent wisdom or supernatural beings.
  • 捣药 - The act of grinding medicine, often associated with immortals or alchemists.
  • 裴真人 - Refers to a legendary figure known for his purity and transcendence, often associated with Daoist traditions.
  • 神仙中人 - Beings from the realm of immortals, often depicted in Chinese mythology and folklore.
  • 仙女 - A fairy, often depicted as a beautiful and ethereal being in Chinese mythology.
  • 鬟髻霓衣 - Describes a fairy's hairstyle and clothing, emphasizing her otherworldly appearance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sequence describes the old woman taking out the medicine and the man immediately beginning to grind it, working during the day and resting at night.
  • This clause describes the old woman taking the mortar and pestle into the inner chamber at night.
  • This sequence describes the man hearing the sound of grinding medicine again and looking to see a jade rabbit holding a mortar and pestle, with the moonlight illuminating the room brightly.
  • This clause describes the old woman taking the medicine and swallowing it, saying she will go into the cave to inform her relatives and prepare a tent for Pai Lang.
  • This sequence describes the old woman taking the woman into the mountain and telling the man to wait a moment, followed by carriages, servants, and attendants welcoming the man.
  • This clause describes the man seeing a grand mansion that reaches the clouds, with pearl doors shining under the sun.
  • This clause describes the interior of the mansion, with luxurious furnishings and treasures, resembling the home of a noble family.
  • This clause describes the man being led into the tent by fairy children and servants, and the ceremony being completed.

  • 诘问 - The accompanying official questioned him. '诘问' is a formal or stern form of questioning.

  • 绣茵锦席，罗列于庭 - '绣茵' means 'embroidered mats,' '锦席' means 'brocade seats,' and '罗列于庭' means 'arranged in the courtyard.' This phrase describes the luxurious furnishings in the courtyard.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 后数年，恕念其女，令其男义方访之 - This sentence introduces the passage and sets up the narrative. It can be read as 'Several years later, Shu, thinking of his daughter, ordered his son Yifang to seek her out.'
  • 到天坛南，道遇一昆仑奴，驾黄牛耕田 - This sequence of actions must be read as a single event: arriving at the southern side of the Altar of Heaven, encountering a black-skinned servant driving a yellow ox to plow the field.
  • 问曰：“此有张老家庄否？”昆仑投杖拜曰：“大郎子何久不来？庄去此甚近，某当前引。”遂与俱东去 - This sequence of dialogue and action must be read as a continuous interaction: asking if there is Zhang Lao's old estate, the servant bowing and responding, and then leading the way east.
  • 初上一山，山下有水，过水连绵凡十余处，景色甚异，不与人间同 - This sequence describes the journey and the scenery: initially climbing a mountain, encountering water at the foot, crossing water that stretches over ten places, and noting the unusual scenery.
  • 忽下一山，水北朱户甲第，楼阁参差，花木繁茂，烟云鲜媚，鸾鹤孔雀，回翔其间 - This sequence describes the sudden descent and the grand estate: descending a mountain, seeing grand mansions with red-painted doors, towers and pavilions of varying heights, lush flowers and trees, fresh and beautiful mist and clouds, and mythical birds soaring among them.
  • 昆仑指曰：“此张家庄也。”韦惊骇不测 - This sequence of dialogue and reaction must be read as a continuous interaction: the servant pointing and identifying the estate, and Wei being greatly surprised and unable to fathom it.
  • 俄而及门，门有紫衣吏引入厅中，铺陈之华，目所未睹，异香氤氲，遍满崖谷 - This sequence describes the arrival at the gate and the grand interior: reaching the gate, encountering a purple-clad official leading them into the hall, seeing splendid decorations, exotic fragrances pervading, and filling the cliffs and valleys.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This question introduces a paradox: why did the mountain spirit, who should have lived long, die instead?
  • This rhetorical question suggests a possible explanation involving the opening of the road to Shu and the use of the transformed woman by the Five Pecks of Rice.
  • This clause presents an alternative explanation, suggesting that the Qin state fabricated a story about gold in cow dung to justify opening a path to Shu.
  • This final clause casts doubt on the accuracy of the traditional story.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 门窗皆扃 - All the doors and windows are locked; indicates the room is sealed off.

  • 冠帔者一人 - One person wearing a hat and a shawl; describes the appearance of the figure.
  • 前逼床抚程微笑 - Approached the bed, touched Cheng, and smiled; shows the figure's approach and interaction.
  • 果熟寝耶 - Are you sound asleep?; rhetorical question indicating the figure's awareness of Cheng's state.
  • 吾非祸人者 - I am not one who brings misfortune; statement of innocence.
  • 与子有宿缘 - I have a predestined connection with you; suggests a fateful or supernatural bond.
  • 何见疑若是 - Why are you so suspicious?; rhetorical question expressing the figure's confusion.
  • 果欲祸吾 - If you really intend to harm me; conditional statement expressing Cheng's fear.
  • 虽卧不起，其可逭乎 - Even if I stay in bed, how can I escape?; rhetorical question expressing Cheng's resignation.
  • 且既彼有宿缘语 - And since it is said we have a predestined connection; conditional statement expressing Cheng's reasoning.
  • 遂推枕下榻 - So he pushed aside the pillow and got off the bed; sequence of actions showing Cheng's response.

  • 下界愚夫 - A foolish man from the lower realms; self-deprecating description.
  • 不知真仙降临 - Unaware that a true immortal has descended; expressing Cheng's ignorance.

  • 诚合万死 - Truly deserving a thousand deaths; expressing Cheng's self-punishment.
  • 美人引手握程起 - The beautiful woman held out her hand and helped Cheng up; sequence of actions showing the figure's kindness.
  • 慰令无惧 - Comforted him to not be afraid; expressing the figure's reassurance.
  • 遂与南面同坐 - So they sat facing south together; describes the seating arrangement.
  • 其二人者，东西相向 - The other two people sat facing east and west; describes the seating arrangement.
  • 即命侍女行酒进馔 - Immediately ordered the maidservants to serve wine and food; describes the figure's command.
  • 品物皆生平目所未睹 - All the items were things he had never seen before in his life; describes the novelty of the items.
  • 俄以红玉莲花卮进酒 - Soon, a red jade lotus cup was brought for wine; describes the presentation of the wine.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 酒每一行，必八音齐奏 - Classical expression describing the ritualistic or luxurious setting in which wine is consumed, with music playing at every pour of wine. '八音' refers to the eight categories of musical instruments in ancient China.
  • 声调清和 - Phrase describing the music as clear and harmonious, suggesting a refined and elegant atmosphere.
  • 令人有超凡遗世之想 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'causes one to have the feeling of transcending the mundane world,' suggesting a spiritual or otherworldly experience.
  • 酒阑 - Classical term meaning 'when the wine is finished' or 'at the end of the drinking session.'
  • 郎夫妇可就寝矣 - Polite and formal way of saying 'the husband and wife may now go to sleep.'
  • 遂为褰帏拂枕而去 - Sequence of actions: 'thus they drew back the curtain, smoothed the pillow, and departed,' indicating the departure of the attendants after the couple is ready for sleep.
  • 瞥然不见 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly vanished' or 'disappeared in an instant,' suggesting an abrupt and mysterious disappearance.
  • 门亦尚扃 - Classical expression meaning 'the door was still locked,' indicating that no one had entered or exited through it.
  • 独留同坐美人，相与解衣登榻 - Phrase meaning 'only the lady who had been sitting with him remained, and together they undressed and climbed onto the bed,' indicating a moment of intimacy.
  • 肌肤滑莹，凝脂不若 - Vivid description of the woman's skin: 'smooth and glossy, even finer than fat (凝脂),' suggesting a supernatural or idealized beauty.
  • 侧身就程，丰若有馀，柔若无骨 - Poetic description of the woman's form: 'turning her body toward Cheng, her fullness seemed abundant, and her softness seemed to have no bones,' emphasizing her ethereal beauty and grace.
  • 神魂飘越，莫知所为矣 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'his soul floated away, and he did not know what to do,' indicating a state of awe or confusion.
  • 喜出望外 - Common idiom meaning 'delighted beyond expectation.'
  • 眷程殊厚 - Phrase meaning 'she showed Cheng great affection.'
  • 世间花月之妖，飞走之怪 - Refers to mythical or supernatural beings, such as 'beings of floral and lunar beauty' and 'creatures that fly or move,' often used in classical literature to describe supernatural entities.
  • 见恶 - Phrase meaning 'are seen as evil' or 'are considered wicked.'
  • 不宜漏泄耳 - Cautionary phrase meaning 'should not be revealed' or 'must not be spoken of.'
  • 非仙也，实海神也 - Direct statement meaning 'I am not a fairy, but a sea god.'
  • 邻舍鸡鸣 - Classical expression meaning 'the rooster crowed in the neighbor’s house,' signaling the approach of dawn or the end of the narrative.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 酒每一行，必八音齐奏 - Reflects the luxurious and ritualistic aspects of ancient Chinese banquets, where music accompanied the serving of wine. The '八音' refers to the eight categories of ancient Chinese instruments: metal, stone, silk, bamboo, gourd, clay, leather, and wood.
  • 美人 - Term for a beautiful woman, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe idealized or supernatural female figures.
  • 侍女 - Attendants or female servants, common in the courtly or aristocratic settings of ancient China.
  • 海神 - A sea deity, indicating a supernatural or divine origin of the character. In Chinese mythology, sea gods are often associated with protection, mystery, and power over the ocean.
  • 邻舍鸡鸣 - Common expression in classical Chinese literature to indicate the passage of time or the end of a night, often used to conclude a narrative.

Proper Nouns

  • 程 - A personal name, likely the protagonist of the narrative. Should be kept as 'Cheng' in English.
  • 海神 - Sea god, a title referring to a supernatural being. Should be translated consistently as 'sea god' or 'god of the sea.'
  • 邻舍 - General term meaning 'neighbor's house' or 'the neighbor's residence.' Should be translated as 'the neighbor’s house' or 'the neighbor’s home.'
  • 鸡鸣 - Crowing of the rooster, a classical expression. Should be translated as 'the rooster crowed' or 'the rooster’s crowing.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '昨夜二美人及諸侍女齊到，各致賀詞，盥洗嚴妝，捧擁而出。轉盼間，已失所在。' describes a series of events that occur in quick succession, creating a dreamlike or supernatural atmosphere.
  • The clause '程茫然自失，曰：“豈其夢耶？”然念飲食笑語，交合誓盟之類，皆歷歷明甚，非夢境也。且惑且喜。' captures the protagonist's mixed emotions and the realism of his experiences.
  • The sequence '出就兄室。兄大骇曰：“汝今神采發越，頓異昨日，何也？”程謬言：“年来失志，鄉井無期。昨夕暴寒，愁思殊切，有何快心而神采發越耶？”' describes the protagonist's interaction with his brother, highlighting the brother's confusion and the protagonist's attempt to explain his transformation.
  • The clause '然程亦自觉神思精明，有加于昔。心竊喜之，唯恐不復至也。' emphasizes the protagonist's inner feelings and his desire for continuity.
  • The sequence '至夜，美人忽至，但儀從音樂不如畴昔之盛，彼二人者，亦不復來。美人笑曰：“郎果有心若是，但當終始如一耳。”即命侍女行酒荐馔，珍腆如昨，歡謔諧笑，则有加焉。須臾撤席就枕，雞鳴復去。自後率以為常，殆無虛夕。' describes the repeated encounters with the beautiful woman, highlighting the changes in the setting and the protagonist's consistent experiences.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遂白王曰 - 遂 means 'then' or 'thereupon,' and 白 means 'to inform' or 'to report.' Together, they indicate the speaker is making a formal report to the king.
  • 其私无颇矣 - 其私 means 'her private affairs,' and 无颇 means 'nothing improper.' This phrase suggests that the woman's private life is in order.
  • 不然者，何以宫中暖金合，得在斯人处耶 - 不然者 means 'if not,' 何以 means 'why,' 宫中暖金合 refers to a warm golden box kept in the palace, and 得在斯人处耶 means 'how could it be in this person's possession?' This is a rhetorical question implying that the box being in the person's possession indicates something improper.
  • 王愀然良久 - 愀然 means 'with a grave expression,' and 良久 means 'for a long time.' This describes the king's somber and reflective state.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '赠答极多，不能备记，粗忆数首而已' is elliptical and grammatically compact, indicating a large number of exchanges that cannot all be remembered, with only a few recalled.
  • The sequence '遂相与出门，复驾辎车，送至下庙，乃执手呜咽而别' must be read as a single narrative, describing a group leaving together, driving in a carriage, being taken to a temple, and then saying a tearful farewell.
  • The clause '及至馆，怀中探得瑶镜，金缕结' is elliptical but means that upon arriving at the inn, the protagonist found a jade mirror and a golden-threaded knot in their chest.
  • The clause '夜而失所在，时同侣咸怪警夜有异香' describes the protagonist's disappearance at night, with companions finding it strange and noting an unusual fragrance.
  • The clause '警后使回至庙中，于神座后得一碧笺，乃是小女郎与警书' describes a messenger returning to the temple, finding a green note behind the deity's seat, which is revealed to be a letter from a young girl to the protagonist.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋咸寧中 - The Xinning era (咸寧) is traditionally associated with the Jin dynasty (265–420 CE), not the Song dynasty (960–1279 CE), suggesting a possible historical error or confusion in the original text.
  • 太常卿韩伯子某 - The name '韩伯子' (Han Bozi) may be a fictional or pseudonymous figure, as no historical record of this name exists in the standard annals of the Jin dynasty.
  • 王蕴子某 - Wang Yun (王蕴) was a real historical figure, a high-ranking official in the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 刘耽子某 - Liu Dan (刘耽) was also a real historical figure, a general and official in the Jin dynasty.
  • 蒋山庙 - A temple dedicated to Jiangshan, a deity or ancestral figure associated with the mountain (蒋山) in the region, likely in Jiangnan (southern China).
  • 蒋侯 - A title or honorific for the deity worshipped at Jiangshan Temple, possibly a local god or a historical figure deified.
  • The term '传教' refers to a messenger or intermediary between the divine and the earthly realm in religious or ritual contexts.
  • The term '三牲' refers to the three sacrificial animals used in ancient Chinese religious offerings: ox, sheep, and pig.
  • The term '乞哀' is a ritual expression used in religious or supernatural contexts to request mercy or forgiveness.
  • The term '亲降' describes the deity’s personal appearance, considered a rare and significant event in religious or mythological contexts.
  • The term '并亡' refers to the sudden and simultaneous death of all three individuals, likely a supernatural consequence of their disrespectful behavior toward the deity.

  • 裸其尸而焚之 - 裸：luǒ, means 'to strip' or 'to undress.' 其尸：the body. 焚之：to burn it.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 連枝梓雙 - A metaphor for a couple who are deeply connected, like twin branches of a tree or a pair of mandarin ducks. Symbolizes the enduring bond between Han Peng and his wife, even in death.
  • 雙 - Emphasizes the idea of a pair or couple, reinforcing the theme of unity in death.
  • 南山有烏，北山張羅 - A metaphorical expression where '南山' (South Mountain) and '北山' (North Mountain) represent opposing forces or situations, and '烏' (crow) and '張羅' (snares) symbolize the futility of trying to escape fate. Suggests that despite efforts to avoid capture, one cannot escape their destiny.
  • 烏自高飛，羅當奈何 - Continues the metaphor, emphasizing that even if the crow tries to fly high, the snares will still catch it. Reflects the inevitability of fate and the wife's resignation to her situation.
  • 烏鹊双飞，不乐凤凰 - A metaphor comparing the common couple (乌鹊) to the noble couple (凤凰). The wife is expressing her dissatisfaction with being compared to a lesser pair, as she sees herself as worthy of a higher status.
  • 妾自庶人，不乐君王 - A direct statement of the wife's rejection of the king's advances. She identifies herself as a commoner and refuses to be treated as a royal concubine.
  • 阴腐其衣 - A metaphorical action where the wife secretly weakens her clothing, symbolizing her determination to end her life in a way that allows her to return to her husband. The act of weakening her clothing suggests a desire to die gracefully and honorably.
  • 愿以尸还韩氏而合葬 - A formal expression of the wife's wish to be buried with her husband, emphasizing her commitment to their union even in death.
  • 分埋之 - A command from the king to separate the bodies of Han Peng and his wife, symbolizing the king's attempt to break their bond.
  • 两冢相望 - A metaphorical description of the two graves facing each other, symbolizing the enduring connection between the couple even after death.
  • 忽有梓木生于两冢，根交于下，枝连于上 - A metaphorical description of the trees growing from the graves, symbolizing the reuniting of Han Peng and his wife in death. The intertwining roots and branches represent their unbreakable bond.
  • 又有鸟如鸳鸯，双栖于树，朝暮悲鸣 - A metaphorical description of the mandarin ducks, which are symbols of love and fidelity. Their presence on the tree and their mournful calls emphasize the tragic nature of the couple's story.
  • 此韩凭夫妇精魂也 - A conclusion that the trees and birds are the spirits of Han Peng and his wife, reinforcing the idea that their love transcends death.
  • 故诗云 - A formal introduction to the poem that follows, indicating that the events described are worthy of poetic expression.

  • 盛妆而出 - 'Dressed in her finest, she came out,' a formal expression of preparation and beauty.

  • 历叙吹箫之曲，绣鞋之事 - 'Recited in detail the flute melody and the embroidered shoes incident,' referring to shared dream experiences.

  • 两相表订以证之 - 'They exchanged tokens to confirm it,' showing mutual commitment.
  • 彼此大惊，以为神契 - 'They were greatly surprised, believing it to be a divine match,' indicating a supernatural connection.

  • 《剪灯新话》名《渭塘奇遇传》 - 'This story is known in *Jian Deng Xin Hua* as *Wei Tang Qi Yu Zhuan*,' indicating the source text and its title.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《剪灯新话》 - *Jian Deng Xin Hua* (New Tales Under the Lamp), a collection of romantic and supernatural stories from the Ming Dynasty, authored by Liu E.
  • 渭塘奇遇传 - *Wei Tang Qi Yu Zhuan* (The Strange Encounter at Wei Tang), the specific title of this story within *Jian Deng Xin Hua*.
  • 肆翁 - 'Shopkeeper' or 'shop owner,' a common term in classical Chinese narrative fiction for a character who serves as a guide or host in a dream or supernatural encounter.
  • 神契 - 'Divine match' or 'fate-bound union,' a term used to describe a romantic or marital connection ordained by heaven, a common trope in classical Chinese romance.
  • 紫金碧甸指环 - A ring made of purple gold (likely a term for a specific alloy or color) and green jade (碧甸), indicating luxury and symbolic significance in the narrative.
  • 水晶双鱼扇坠 - A fan pendant made of crystal with two fish, a traditional Chinese motif symbolizing harmony, prosperity, or love.

Proper Nouns

  • 《剪灯新话》 - *Jian Deng Xin Hua* (New Tales Under the Lamp), the title of the classical Chinese collection of stories.
  • 《渭塘奇遇传》 - *Wei Tang Qi Yu Zhuan* (The Strange Encounter at Wei Tang), the title of this particular story within *Jian Deng Xin Hua*.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 因省试下第，薄游东海 - Failing the imperial examinations after provincial tests, leading to a light journey to the East Sea.
  • 值吴女盈盈者来 - Encountering or happening to meet the young woman named Yingying from the state of Wu.
  • 年才十六，善歌舞，尤工弹筝 - Aged only sixteen, skilled in singing and dancing, especially proficient in playing the zither.
  • 少年子争登其门，不惜金帛 - Young men vying to enter her door, not sparing gold and silk, indicating her popularity and wealth.
  • 盈遴简佳偶，乃许一笑 - Carefully selecting a good match, granting a smile, showing her discerning nature.
  • 府守田龙图召使侍宴 - The governor with the title Longtu inviting her to a banquet.
  • 山预宾列，相得于樽俎之间 - Wang Shan being among the guests, getting along well during the banquet.
  • 从之欢处累月 - Spending many months in joy with her.
  • 山辞归，盈垂泣悲啼，不能自止 - Wang Shan bidding farewell, Yingying weeping and sobbing, unable to stop.
  • 明年，寄《伤春曲》示山 - Sending the 'Spring Sorrow' poem to Wang Shan the following year.
  • 芳菲时节，花压枝折 - Fragrant and beautiful spring season, flowers pressing down the branches.
  • 蜂蝶撩乱，阑槛光发 - Bees and butterflies disturbed, the railings and gates shining brightly.
  • 一旦碎花魂，葬花骨 - Shattering the soul of the flowers, burying the bones of the flowers, symbolizing the end of spring beauty.
  • 蜂兮蝶兮何不来？空使雕阑对寒月 - Why don't the bees and butterflies come? Leaving the carved railings facing the cold moon, expressing a sense of loneliness.
  • 芳菲时节，花压枝折 - Repeated for emphasis, reinforcing the initial description of spring.
  • 龙脑透缕鲛绡红，鸳鸯十二罗芙蓉 - Dragon brain incense permeating the threads of red silken fabric, twelve mandarin ducks on lotus flowers, indicating luxurious and intricate embroidery.
  • 盈盈初见十五六，眉试青膏鬓垂绿 - First meeting when she was fifteen or sixteen, eyebrows painted with green powder and hair hanging green, describing her appearance.
  • 道字不正娇满怀，学得襄阳大堤曲 - Her pronunciation not quite right, but full of charm, having learned the songs of the Great Dyke of Xiangyang, indicating her playful and learned nature.
  • 阿母偏怜掌上看，自此风流难管束 - Mother especially fond of watching her, from now on, her charm is hard to control, showing the mother's affection and the daughter's growing independence.
  • 莺啄含桃未咽时，便会吟诗风动竹 - Oriole pecking at the apricot before swallowing, already reciting poetry, making the wind move the bamboo, describing her early talent.
  • 日高一丈罗窗（目+歎左），啼鸟压 - Sun high, embroidered window open, singing birds pressing down, creating a serene morning scene.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏人王山 - Wang Shan, a person from the state of Wei, indicating his regional origin.
  • 省试下第 - Failing the imperial examinations after provincial tests, a significant setback in a scholar's life.
  • 东海 - Refers to the East Sea, a geographical region in ancient China.
  • 吴女盈盈 - Yingying, a young woman from the state of Wu, indicating her place of origin and personal identity.
  • 弹筝 - Playing the zither, a traditional Chinese musical instrument highly valued in classical Chinese culture.
  • 词翰情思 - Literary works and emotional depth, indicating her intellectual and artistic qualities.
  • 少年子争登其门，不惜金帛 - Indicates the high social status and popularity of women in classical Chinese society.
  • 府守田龙图 - The governor with the title Longtu, a high-ranking official.
  • 樽俎之间 - The table and wine cups, symbolizing a formal setting.
  • 伤春曲 - A type of classical Chinese poetry expressing the sorrow of spring, often associated with themes of loss and beauty.
  • 芳菲时节 - Refers to the spring season, a time of beauty and renewal.
  • 龙脑透缕鲛绡红，鸳鸯十二罗芙蓉 - Describes luxurious and intricate embroidery, indicating high social status and artistic refinement.
  • 襄阳大堤曲 - Refers to a famous song from the Great Dyke of Xiangyang, a historical and cultural reference.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 花新睡短 - Poetic expression suggesting the woman is newly awakened or has just risen from sleep, with a sense of fragility or delicacy.
  • 腻云纤指拢还偏 - Metaphorical and poetic description of her fingers gently adjusting her hair, which is still slightly disheveled.
  • 半被可怜留翠暖 - Metaphor for her lingering in bed or the lingering warmth of her presence.
  • 淡黄衫袖仙衣轻 - Description of her clothing, suggesting a delicate, almost ethereal appearance.
  • 红玉栏杆妆粉浅 - Poetic description of her makeup and surroundings; '红玉栏杆' may be a metaphor for red jade railings or a poetic way to describe the setting.
  • 酒痕落腮梅忍寒 - Metaphor combining the natural imagery of plum blossoms with the redness of wine on her face.
  • 春羞入眼横波艳 - Poetic way of describing her eyes ('横波' = 'flowing waves,' a classical term for eyes with a gentle, alluring gaze) and the shyness of spring.
  • 一缕未消山枕红 - A trace of red remains on the mountain-shaped pillow, referring to the lingering effects of her presence or the red of her lipstick or makeup.
  • 斜睇整衣移步懒 - She glances sideways, adjusts her clothes, and moves slowly, suggesting a shy or reluctant movement.
  • 才如韩寿潘安亚 - Her talent is as great as Han Shou and Pan An, referencing two famous handsome and talented men of antiquity.
  • 掷果窃香心暗嫁 - She throws fruit and secretly steals fragrance, her heart secretly betrothed. A reference to the story of Pan An, used metaphorically to suggest secret affection or courtship.
  • 小花静院酒阑珊 - In the quiet courtyard with small flowers, the wine is fading, suggesting a quiet, intimate setting.
  • 别有私言银烛下 - There are private words spoken under the silver candlelight, indicating a secret or intimate conversation.
  • 帘声浪皱金泥额 - The curtain's sound ripples like waves, wrinkling the golden-painted forehead. A poetic description of the movement of the curtain and its effect on the woman’s appearance.
  • 六尺牙床罗帐窄 - The six-foot bed with silk curtains is narrow, describing the setting and perhaps her feelings of confinement or intimacy.
  • 钗横啼笑两不分 - Her hairpin is askew, and she cannot tell whether she is weeping or laughing, a metaphor for emotional confusion or turmoil.
  • 历尽风波腰一捻 - Having weathered many storms, her waist is now slender, suggesting that she has endured hardship and has become more refined or delicate.
  • 若教飞上九天歌 - If she were to sing in the nine heavens, a poetic metaphor for her voice or beauty being so great it could reach the heavens.
  • 一声自可倾人国 - One note would be enough to captivate a nation, a hyperbolic expression of her beauty or voice.
  • 娇多必是春工与 - Her excessive tenderness must be the work of spring, suggesting that her beauty is a product of nature or springtime.
  • 有能动人情几许 - How much emotion can she move in others? A rhetorical question about her ability to stir the hearts of others.
  • 前年按舞使君筵 - Last year at the banquet of the magistrate, she performed a dance, referring to a past performance or event.
  • 眸蹙忍羞头不举 - Her eyes furrowed, she held back her shame, not lifting her head, a description of shyness or humiliation.
  • 凤凰箫冷曲成迟 - The phoenix flute is cold, and the melody is slow to form, a metaphor for a melancholy or delayed performance.
  • 凝醉挑花过风雨 - Drunk and dazed, she plucks flowers through wind and rain, suggesting a state of emotional or physical turmoil.
  • 阿盈阿盈听我语 - Ah Ying, Ah Ying, listen to my words, a direct address to the woman, possibly a nickname or a poetic name.
  • 劝君休向阳台住 - I advise you not to stay at the Pavilion of the Clouds, a reference to a romantic or mythical setting (阳台 = Pavilion of the Clouds), possibly alluding to a story of love or separation.
  • 一生纵得楚王怜 - Even if she were loved by King Chu for a lifetime, referencing the famous story of the beauty of the Lady of Xiang, who was loved by King Chu.
  • 宋玉才多谁解赋 - Though Cao Yu (宋玉) has much talent, who can understand her poetry? Cao Yu is a famous poet, and this line may be suggesting that even a great poet cannot fully capture her beauty or talent.
  • 洛阳无限青楼女 - In Luoyang, there are countless courtesans, indicating the abundance of beautiful women in the city.
  • 袖拢红牙金凤缕 - She rolls up her sleeves, revealing red teeth and golden phoenix patterns, a poetic and possibly metaphorical description of her attire or features.
  • 春衫粉面谁家郎 - Who is the young man in a spring robe with a powdered face? A description of a romantic partner or a question about identity.
  • 只把黄金买歌舞 - He only buys songs and dances with gold, suggesting that the young man is wealthy but perhaps shallow or indulgent.
  • 就中薄倖五陵儿 - Among them, the unfaithful boys of the Five Tombs, referring to young men from the Five Tombs area, known for their wealth and indulgence.
  • 一日心冷玉如土 - One day, when his heart turns cold, he is as worthless as dirt, a metaphor for the fleeting nature of love or wealth.
  • 云零雨散那堪悲 - The clouds disperse, the rain ceases—how can one bear this sorrow? A metaphor for the end of a relationship or a romantic affair.
  • 空入他人梦来去 - In vain, she enters the dreams of others, coming and going, suggesting unrequited love or a love that is not returned.
  • 浣花溪上海棠湾 - At the banks of the Huanhua Creek, the bay of crabapple trees, a geographical reference to the location of the poet or the scene.
  • 薛涛朱户皆金环 - Xue Tao’s red doors are all adorned with golden rings, referring to the poetess Xue Tao and her luxurious surroundings.
  • 韦皋笔逸玳瑁落 - Wei Gao’s brush is free-flowing, and the tortoiseshell falls, a metaphor for the elegance and skill of Wei Gao’s writing.
  • 张祜盏滑琉璃干 - Zhang Hu’s cup is smooth, and the glass is dry, a poetic way of describing the emptiness of a cup or the end of a drinking session.
  • 压倒念奴价百倍 - She surpasses Nian Nu in value by a hundredfold, referencing Nian Nu, a famous singer from the Tang Dynasty.
  • 兴来奇怪生毫端 - When inspiration strikes, strange and wondrous things arise from the tip of the brush, a metaphor for poetic creativity.
  • 醉眸 - Drunk eyes, indicating a state of intoxication or emotional intoxication.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 韩寿潘安 - Han Shou and Pan An were two famous handsome and talented men of the Jin Dynasty. Pan An is especially well-known for his beauty and the legend that women would throw fruit at him as he walked.
  • 掷果窃香 - A direct reference to the legend of Pan An, who was so handsome that women would throw fruit at him as he passed by.
  • 小花静院 - A poetic description of a quiet, intimate setting, possibly a garden or courtyard.
  • 银烛 - Refers to silver or white candles, often used in classical poetry to describe romantic or intimate settings.
  • 钗横 - A term used to describe disheveled hairpins, often associated with moments of emotional disturbance or intimacy.
  • 山枕 - A type of pillow shaped like a mountain, often used in classical poetry to describe the setting or the softness of the bed.
  • 九天 - A term referring to the heavens or the highest level of the cosmos, often used in poetry to describe extraordinary beauty or power.
  • 春工 - A poetic term for the work of spring, often used to describe the natural beauty or the influence of spring.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 觑纸聊一扫 - Playful expression meaning 'to glance at the paper and casually brush it away,' suggesting a carefree or dismissive attitude toward the act of writing.
  • 落花飞雪声漫漫 - Poetic phrase evoking the sound of falling flowers and snowflakes, symbolizing the passage of time or the ephemeral nature of life.
  • 梦得见之为改观 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'Dreams see it as a new perspective,' suggesting that the act of writing transforms the writer's perception.
  • 乐天更敢寻常看 - Reference to Bai Juyi (白居易), meaning 'Bai Juyi dares to look at it in an ordinary way,' implying a sense of humility or simplicity in his approach to writing.
  • 花开不肯下翠幕 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'Flowers bloom but refuse to lower the green curtain,' symbolizing the persistence of beauty or vitality.
  • 竟日煊赫罗雕鞍 - Vivid description of the sun shining brightly on ornate saddles, suggesting a scene of opulence or grandeur.
  • 扫眉涂粉迨七十 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'To sweep the eyebrows and paint the face until seventy,' referring to the act of writing or creating art well into old age.
  • 老大始顶菖蒲冠 - Reference to the practice of wearing a *cāngpú guān* (菖蒲冠), a type of hat made from *cāngpú* (菖蒲), a plant associated with longevity and immortality. The phrase suggests that the writer only begins this practice in old age.
  • 至今愁人锦江口 - Melancholic expression meaning 'Even now, the person grieves at the mouth of the Jinjiang River,' evoking a sense of sorrow or longing.
  • 秋蛩露草孤坟寒 - Poetic phrase describing the cold, desolate scene of a lonely grave in autumn, with the sound of autumn insects and dew-covered grass.
  • 盈盈大雅真可惜 - Lamentation meaning 'The graceful and refined beauty is truly a pity,' expressing regret or sorrow over the loss of something valuable.
  • 尔生此后不可得 - Somber expression meaning 'After you are born, it cannot be obtained,' suggesting the irreversible nature of time or loss.
  • 满天风月独倚阑 - Poetic phrase meaning 'Under the full sky of wind and moon, leaning alone on the railing,' evoking a sense of solitude and contemplation.
  • 醉岸深云呼佚墨 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'Drunk, calling out to the lost ink in the deep clouds,' suggesting a call to creativity or inspiration.
  • 久之不见子心忆 - Melancholic expression meaning 'After a long time, not seeing you, my heart remembers,' expressing longing or nostalgia.
  • 高玑去天无几尺 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'The high *gaoji* (a celestial object) is only a few feet from the sky,' suggesting the proximity of something divine or distant.
  • 斜阳衔山云半红 - Poetic phrase describing the sunset, with the sun setting behind the mountains and the clouds turning red, evoking a sense of beauty and transience.
  • 远水无风天一碧 - Poetic phrase describing the distant water and sky, both appearing as one clear blue, evoking a sense of vastness and tranquility.
  • 望眼空遥沉翠翼 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'Looking into the distance, the eyes sink into the green wings,' suggesting a longing for something distant or unreachable.
  • 银河易阔天南北 - Poetic phrase meaning 'The Milky Way easily spans the north and south of the sky,' evoking a sense of vastness and the separation between heaven and earth.
  • 瘦尽休文带眼移 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'The belt of Xiuwen (a reference to the poet Bao Zhao) has shifted, indicating his emaciation,' suggesting the toll of time or sorrow on the body.
  • 忍向小楼清泪滴 - Melancholic expression meaning 'How can one bear to cry clear tears on a small building,' expressing deep sorrow or heartbreak.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 梦得 - Reference to Liu Yuxi (刘禹锡), a famous Tang Dynasty poet, often paired with Bai Juyi.
  • 乐天 - Reference to Bai Juyi (白居易), a famous Tang Dynasty poet, known for his simple and accessible style.
  • 盈盈 - Poetic term often used to describe graceful or delicate beauty, possibly referring to a person or a poetic ideal.
  • 菖蒲冠 - Type of hat made from *cāngpú* (菖蒲), a plant associated with longevity and immortality in Chinese culture.
  • 锦江 - Famous river in Chengdu, known for its scenic beauty and cultural significance.
  • 盈盈大雅 - Reference to the graceful and refined beauty of a person or a poetic ideal.
  • 高玑 - Celestial object, possibly referring to a star or a comet, often associated with the divine or the distant.
  • 休文 - Reference to Bao Zhao (鲍照), a famous poet of the Southern Dynasties, known for his melancholic and introspective works.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 烛花红死睡初醒 - 烛花 refers to the flame of a candle; 红死 suggests the flame has dimmed or died; 睡初醒 means just waking from sleep; evokes the image of waking up in the early morning when the candle flame is about to go out.
  • 一枕孤怀病客情 - 一枕 means a pillow, symbolizing sleep or rest; 孤怀 refers to a lonely or isolated state of mind; 病客情 describes the feelings of a sick or weary traveler; conveys the loneliness and weariness of a traveler in poor health.
  • 海上有仙应入梦 - 海上有仙 refers to the myth of immortals living in the sea; 应入梦 means should enter the dream; suggests that the dreamer is longing for the company of immortals or a spiritual escape.
  • 人间无路可藏生 - 人间 means the human world; 无路可藏生 suggests that there is no way to hide or escape from life's hardships; conveys a sense of hopelessness or resignation.
  • 乾坤眼阔成新恨 - 乾坤 refers to the universe or the world; 眼阔 means wide-eyed or broad-minded; 成新恨 means forming new grievances; suggests that the vastness of the world has led to new feelings of sorrow or anger.
  • 风月人归似旧情 - 风月 refers to the beauty of nature or the passage of time; 人归 means people return; 似旧情 means like old feelings; suggests that despite the passage of time, the feelings remain unchanged.
  • 汉殿香消春寂寂 - 汉殿 refers to the palace of the Han dynasty; 香消 means the fragrance fades; 春寂寂 means the spring is quiet; evokes a sense of melancholy as the spring season passes and the palace becomes silent.
  • 夕阳无雨下西城 - 夕阳 means sunset; 无雨 means no rain; 下西城 means falling over the western city; describes a peaceful sunset over the western city, with no rain to disturb the scene.
  • 浮世繁华一梦休 - 浮世 refers to the transient nature of life; 繁华 means prosperity or luxury; 一梦休 means a dream ends; suggests that the transient pleasures of life are like a dream that has come to an end.
  • 登临因忆昔年游 - 登临 means to ascend or climb; 因忆 means because of remembering; 昔年游 means past travels; suggests that the act of ascending a mountain or high place brings back memories of past journeys.
  • 人归依旧野花笑 - 人归 means people return; 依旧 means still or as before; 野花笑 means wildflowers laugh; suggests that despite the passage of time, nature remains unchanged and vibrant.
  • 玉冷几经坟树秋 - 玉冷 refers to something cold and hard, possibly a metaphor for the heart; 几经 means after several times or after many times; 坟树秋 means tomb trees in autumn; suggests that the heart has grown cold after experiencing many losses and the passage of time.
  • 风月遇清须感慨 - 风月 refers to the beauty of nature; 遇清 means encountering clarity or meeting purity; 须感慨 means must feel moved; suggests that encountering the beauty of nature should evoke a sense of awe or reflection.
  • 江山多恨即迟留 - 江山 refers to the land or the country; 多恨 means full of grievances; 即迟留 means immediately linger or stay longer; suggests that the land is filled with sorrow, and one should linger there to reflect on these sorrows.
  • 如今纵拟夸才思 - 如今 means now; 纵拟 means even if one intends; 夸才思 means to boast of one's talent; suggests that even if one tries to boast of their talent now, it is futile.
  • 事往情多特地愁 - 事往 means things have passed; 情多 means feelings are many; 特地愁 means especially sad; suggests that the more things have passed, the more one feels sad.
  • 柳条黄尽杏梢新 - 柳条黄尽 means willow branches turn yellow; 杏梢新 means apricot branches are new; describes the changing seasons, with willow branches turning yellow and apricot branches budding new leaves.
  • 山翠无非昔日春 - 山翠 means green mountains; 无非 means no different from; 昔日春 means past spring; suggests that the green mountains are no different from the past spring, indicating the persistence of nature.
  • 花色笑风春似醉 - 花色 means flower color; 笑风 means laughing in the wind; 春似醉 means spring seems drunk; suggests that the flowers, swaying in the wind, seem intoxicated by the beauty of spring.
  • 寂寥惟少赏花人 - 寂寥 means lonely or solitary; 惟少 means only few; 赏花人 means flower admirers; suggests that despite the beauty of the flowers, there are few people to appreciate them.
  • 一枝红拂牡丹徽 - 一枝 means a branch; 红拂 means red brush; 牡丹徽 means peony mark; suggests that a red brush has touched the peony, leaving a mark.
  • 无端不入襄王梦 - 无端 means without reason; 不入 means not entering; 襄王梦 refers to the dream of King Xiang of Chu, who dreamed of a beautiful woman; suggests that something has no reason to enter the dream of King Xiang.
  • 为雨为云到处飞 - 为雨为云 means turning into rain and clouds; 到处飞 means flying everywhere; suggests that something is transforming into rain and clouds and flying everywhere, possibly referring to a spirit or a dream.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 烛花 - Refers to the flame of a candle, often used in classical Chinese poetry to symbolize the passage of time or the fleeting nature of life.
  • 海上有仙 - Refers to the mythological idea of immortals living in the sea, a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 汉殿 - Refers to the palace of the Han dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history known for its cultural and political achievements.
  • 夕阳 - The sunset is often used in Chinese poetry to symbolize the end of the day, the passage of time, or the end of an era.
  • 浮世 - Refers to the transient and illusory nature of life, a concept that appears in various forms of Chinese literature and philosophy.
  • 风月 - Refers to the beauty of nature or the passage of time, often used in poetry to evoke a sense of nostalgia or reflection.
  • 江山 - Refers to the land or the country, often used in poetry to express national pride or sorrow over the state of the nation.
  • 柳条黄尽杏梢新 - Describes the changing seasons, with willow branches turning yellow and apricot branches budding new leaves, a common motif in Chinese poetry.
  • 牡丹 - The peony is a symbol of wealth, honor, and beauty in Chinese culture, often used in poetry and art.
  • 襄王梦 - Refers to the dream of King Xiang of Chu, who dreamed of a beautiful woman, a story often used in literature to symbolize unattainable desires or dreams.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 烛花红死睡初醒，一枕孤怀病客情 - The sentence is elliptical, with the subject implied. It can be read as 'When the candle flame dies and one wakes from sleep, one is left with a lonely heart and the feelings of a weary traveler.'
  • 海上有仙应入梦，人间无路可藏生 - The sentence contrasts the dream of immortals with the harsh reality of life, suggesting that there is no escape from the difficulties of the human world.
  • 乾坤眼阔成新恨，风月人归似旧情 - The sentence contrasts the vastness of the world with the persistence of old feelings, suggesting that the vastness of the world has led to new feelings of sorrow or anger, while old feelings remain unchanged.
